name: Two Sum
title: Two Sum
description:
Given an array of integers nums and an integer target, return indices of the two numbers such that they add up to target.

You may assume that each input would have exactly one solution, and you may not use the same element twice.

You can return the answer in any order.

Example 1:
Input: nums = [2,7,11,15], target = 9
Output: [0,1]
Explanation: Because nums[0] + nums[1] == 9, we return [0, 1].

Example 2:
Input: nums = [3,2,4], target = 6
Output: [1,2]

Example 3:
Input: nums = [3,3], target = 6
Output: [0,1]

Constraints:
• 2 <= nums.length <= 10^4
• -10^9 <= nums[i] <= 10^9
• -10^9 <= target <= 10^9
• Only one valid answer exists.
explanation:
{"io": [{"input": " nums = [2,7,11,15], target = 9", "output": "[0,1]", "explanation": "Because nums[0] + nums[1] == 9, we return [0, 1]"}, {"input": "nums = [3,2,4], target = 6", "output": "[1,2]", "explanation": ""}, {"input": "nums = [3,3], target = 6", "output": " [0,1]", "explanation": ""}], "note": "Follow-up: Can you come up with an algorithm that is less than O(n2) time complexity?", "tips": "
  • Use a hashmap to achieve O(1) average lookup time and overall O(n) complexity.
  • Store indices as values in the hashmap so they can be returned immediately.
  • Always check for the complement before inserting the current number to avoid using the same index twice.
  • Be careful with duplicate values; the algorithm handles them naturally when implemented correctly.
  • For performance-critical systems, avoid nested loops—they increase complexity to O(n²).
  • Write tests that include edge cases such as empty arrays, negatives, and duplicates.
", "steps": "
  1. Initialize an empty hashmap seen = {} to store value → index mappings.
  2. Iterate over the array with index i and value v.
  3. Compute the complement as target - v.
  4. If the complement exists in seen, return the pair of indices.
  5. If not, store the current value and index in seen.
  6. Continue until a valid pair is found or return null if none exists.
", "useCase": "
  • Financial systems: Find two transactions that match a target threshold.
  • Real-time monitoring: Detect paired events that together reach a critical metric.
  • Data analytics: Quickly answer queries like “Which two values reach this total?”
  • Gaming: Calculate combinations of items or resources to reach a specific total.
  • System design: Demonstrates efficient hashing strategies for lookup-heavy tasks.
", "constraints": ["2 <= nums.length <= 104", "-109 <= nums[i] <= 109", "-109 <= target <= 109", "Only one valid answer exists."], "problemStatement": "

Given an array of integers nums and an integer target, return indices of the two numbers such that they add up to target.

You may assume that each input would have exactly one solution, and you may not use the same element twice. Return [-1,-1] if no target matched.

Follow the algorithm visually with traces and animated steps.
For better clarity, open the visualization below.

implementations:
[{"code": [{"code": "function twoSum(nums: number[], target: number): number[] { // Hash map to store number and its index const seen = new Map(); for (let i = 0; i < nums.length; i++) { const complement = target - nums[i]; // Check if complement exists in hash map if (seen.has(complement)) { return [seen.get(complement)!, i]; } // Store current number and its index seen.set(nums[i], i); } return [-1,-1]; // No solution found }", "codeType": "optimize", "explanationAfter": "", "explanationBefore": "

Intuition:
Imagine you have a list of numbers and a target value, like wanting two prices that together make ₹10. As you look at each number, you ask yourself: "What number do I need to pair with this to reach the target?"

For example, if the number is 3, you need 7. You then check: "Have I already seen 7 before?" If yes — you found your answer. If not — you simply remember the 3 and move on.

Steps-by-step thinking:

You're basically doing this repeatedly:
1. Look at the current number.
2. Calculate the missing number needed to reach the target.
3. Check if that missing number was seen earlier.
4. If seen → return the two positions. If not → remember this number.

This method is fast because you don't compare every pair manually. Instead, you store what you've seen and check instantly if the matching partner exists.

To check this efficiently, we use a hash map seen where the key is a number and the value is its index. This allows constant-time lookup, turning the problem from a slow O(n²) search into a fast O(n) single pass.

Time Complexity: O(n)
Space Complexity: O(n)

Learn Complexity

", "showExplanationBefore": true}, {"code": "function twoSum(nums: number[], target: number): number[] { const n = nums.length; // Pick the first number for (let i = 0; i < n; i++) { // Pair it with every number after it for (let j = i + 1; j < n; j++) { // Check if the sum matches the target if (nums[i] + nums[j] === target) { return [i, j]; } } } // No solution found as prpblem stated return [-1,-1]; } ", "codeType": "brute-force", "isVisible": true, "explanationAfter": "", "explanationBefore": "

Intuition:
The brute force approach means trying every possible pair of numbers until you find two that add up to the target. There are no shortcuts or memory used — just plain checking.

Imagine pairing each number with every other number, similar to how everyone in a room might shake hands with everyone else. For each pair, you check whether their sum equals the target value.

Step-by-step thinking:

  • Pick the first number in the array.
  • Add it to every number that comes after it.
  • If the sum matches the target, return their indices.
  • If not, move to the next number and repeat the process.

This approach does not store or remember any previous results. It simply checks all combinations until a solution is found.

Why this is slow:
As the array grows larger, the number of comparisons increases very quickly. For large inputs, this approach becomes impractical.

Time Complexity: O(n²)
Space Complexity: O(1)

Learn Complexity

"}, {"code": "function twoSum(nums: number[], target: number): number[] { const seen = new Map(); // First pass: store all numbers with their indices for (let i = 0; i < nums.length; i++) { seen.set(nums[i], i); } // Second pass: find the complement for (let i = 0; i < nums.length; i++) { const complement = target - nums[i]; // Ensure we don't use the same element twice if (seen.has(complement) && seen.get(complement)! !== i) { return [i, seen.get(complement)!]; } } // No solution found (safety fallback) return [-1,-1]; } ", "codeType": "hash-slow", "explanationAfter": "

Comparison of approaches

Approach Core Idea Time Complexity Space Complexity When to Use Notes
Brute Force Try all possible pairs O(n²) O(1) Learning basics, very small input Simple but very slow
Sorting + Two Pointers Sort then move pointers inward O(n log n) O(n) When hashing is restricted Indices must be preserved
Sorting + Binary Search Search complement after sorting O(n log n) O(n) Alternative to two pointers Slightly slower than two pointers
Two-Pass Hash (Slow Hash) Store first, search later O(n) O(n) Beginner-friendly hashing Clear but not optimal
One-Pass Hash (Optimal) Store & search together O(n) O(n) Production & interviews Fastest and most common
", "explanationBefore": "

Intuition:
The two-pass hash (slow hash) approach works by first remembering all the numbers and their positions. Once everything is stored, we scan the array again to find the two numbers that add up to the target.

Instead of checking every possible pair, we store each number in a hash map. This allows us to quickly check whether the required matching number already exists.

Step-by-step thinking:

  • Create an empty hash map to store numbers and their indices.
  • Loop through the array and store each number with its index.
  • Loop through the array again.
  • For each number, calculate the complement (target - current).
  • Check if the complement exists in the hash map.
  • Ensure the complement is not the same index as the current number.
  • If found, return both indices.

This approach uses extra memory to avoid unnecessary comparisons. By trading space for speed, it significantly reduces the number of operations compared to brute force.

Why this is faster:
Instead of comparing every pair, the hash map allows constant-time lookups. Even though the array is scanned twice, the total work grows linearly with the input size.

Time Complexity: O(n)
Space Complexity: O(n)

Learn Complexity

{"likes": 1, "dislikes": 0, "listType": "blind75", "overview": "

The Two Sum problem requires finding two numbers in an array that add up to a given target. A brute-force approach checks all possible pairs, but this becomes inefficient for large inputs.

A more optimized approach uses a hashmap to store previously seen numbers. For each value, we compute the complement (target - current) and look it up in constant time. This eliminates the need for nested loops and significantly improves performance.

The algorithm completes in linear time and is widely used in scenarios like real-time lookups, financial transaction checks, and fast query systems where quick pairing logic is essential.
